edges.description: Toxigenic strains can produce superantigenic exotoxins that drive the scarlet fever rash.
Untreated or delayed treatment of scarlet fever can permit local and systemic sequelae.
Toxin-mediated immune activation produces the diffuse erythematous rash.
Toxigenic emm lineages contribute superantigen profiles that feed the toxin-mediated scarlet fever mechanism.

nodes.description: Cephalosporins can be used for non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy, while clindamycin and macrolides are alternatives for immediate-type penicillin hypersensitivity.

evidence.snippet: For patients who have a non-anaphylactic allergy to penicillin, oral cephalosporin is an acceptable alternative. For patients with a history of immediate, anaphylactic-type hypersensitivity to penicillin, oral clindamycin, clarithromycin, and azithromycin are acceptable alternatives.

nodes.description: Penicillin or amoxicillin therapy treats group A streptococcal infection, reduces symptom duration, and helps prevent immune-mediated complications.

evidence.snippet: Antimicrobial therapy should be initiated without delay once the diagnosis is confirmed. Oral penicillin V and amoxicillin remain the drugs of choice.
Antibiotics should be prescribed early to mitigate its duration, sequelae, and community spread.

nodes.description: Recent scarlet fever and GAS surveillance has identified changing emm-type distributions, including emm12, emm1, and M1UK lineages with distinctive superantigen gene profiles.

biological_processes: biological process involved in interaction with host

evidence.snippet: Sixteen emm types were identified with predominance of emm12 (66.4%) and emm1 (29.8%).
Four novel M1UK isolates were found in Shanghai, with distinctive characteristics of presence of speC and ssa.

nodes.description: Fever is a common systemic manifestation of scarlet fever.

evidence.snippet: Superantigens (SAgs) secreted by this Group A streptococcus (GAS) usually overstimulate the human immune system, causing an amplified hypersensitivity reaction leading to initial symptoms such as sore throat, high fever, and a sandpaper-like skin rash.

nodes.description: Scarlet fever commonly occurs with streptococcal pharyngitis.

evidence.snippet: Children with GABHS pharyngitis typically present with an abrupt onset of fever, intense pain in the throat, pain on swallowing, an inflamed pharynx, enlarged and erythematous tonsils, a red and swollen uvula, enlarged tender anterior cervical lymph nodes.

nodes.description: Scarlet fever can be followed by clinically important local and systemic sequelae, including acute rheumatic fever, endocarditis, and glomerulonephritis, which motivates early diagnosis and treatment.

evidence.snippet: The early diagnosis and treatment of this disease is critical to obviate the development of local and systemic sequelae such as acute rheumatic fever, endocarditis, and glomerulonephritis.

nodes.description: A diffuse erythematous scarlatiniform rash is the defining rash phenotype.

evidence.snippet: Superantigens (SAgs) secreted by this Group A streptococcus (GAS) usually overstimulate the human immune system, causing an amplified hypersensitivity reaction leading to initial symptoms such as sore throat, high fever, and a sandpaper-like skin rash.

nodes.description: Streptococcus pyogenes colonizes and infects the upper respiratory tract, producing acute pharyngitis with fever and systemic inflammatory symptoms.

evidence.snippet: Group A ß-hemolytic Streptococcus (GABHS) is the leading bacterial cause of acute pharyngitis in children and adolescents worldwide.

nodes.description: Streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxins act as superantigens and trigger immune activation, producing the characteristic scarlatiniform rash and mucosal findings of scarlet fever.

biological_processes: biological process involved in interaction with host

evidence.snippet: Superantigens (SAgs) secreted by this Group A streptococcus (GAS) usually overstimulate the human immune system, causing an amplified hypersensitivity reaction leading to initial symptoms such as sore throat, high fever, and a sandpaper-like skin rash.
GAS promotes severe inflammation through mechanisms involving inflammasomes, IL-1β, and T-cell hyperactivation.
